1: Psalm 38 belongs to the traditional seven penitential psalms of the church (Pss 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143), used in Christian liturgy for Ash Wednesday, Holy Week, and seasons of penitence. The superscription lĕhazkîr (as a memorial offering) is unique among the psalm headings — it appears also in Ps 70 (which overlaps with Psalms 40:13-17). The mazkîr offering was the memorial portion of the grain offering (minḥāh), the portion burned on the altar as a "remembrance before the LORD" (Leviticus 2:2, 9, 16; Psalms 5:12; Psalms 6:15; Numbers 5:26). By connecting this prayer to the memorial offering, the superscription frames the psalm as a liturgical act of bringing the speaker's suffering and sin "before God's face" in the same way the incense smoke of the altar rose as a memorial.
2: "Your arrows have pierced me deeply (nĕḥatû bî ḥiṣṣêkā), and your hand has come down hard on me" — the image of divine arrows as instruments of judgment appears throughout the OT and ANE: in the Ugaritic texts, Baal fights with lightning-bolt arrows; Psalms 7:12-13 describes YHWH as an archer preparing arrows against the wicked; in Job 6:4 Job complains "the arrows of the Almighty are in me." The arrows and the heavy hand are two registers of the same experience: precise targeted impact (arrows) and crushing weight (hand). Together they convey that the suffering is experienced as divinely directed, not random misfortune.
3: "My body has no soundness (ʾên-šālôm) because of your anger; my bones have no rest because of my sin" — the connection between physical illness and divine anger/sin is explicitly stated here, reflecting the ancient Israelite understanding of the body as a psychosomatic unity in which moral and physical states are intertwined. Deuteronomy 28 lists a catalog of diseases (Psalms 38:22, 27-28, 35, 60-61) as covenant curses for disobedience. ANE medicine was not separated from religion: the Babylonian bārû (divination priest) and the Assyrian āšipu (exorcist) diagnosed illness as the result of sin or offense against the deity and prescribed both medical and ritual responses. This is not naive — it reflects a world-view in which physical, moral, and spiritual reality are unified.
4: "My iniquities have overwhelmed me (ʿābrû rōʾšî) — they are a burden too heavy to carry (kĕmassāʾ kābēd yiḵbĕdû mimenî)" — the weight metaphor for guilt is physically visceral. ʿĀbar rōʾš (to pass over the head) is used elsewhere for floodwaters passing over someone (Psalms 69:2; Isaiah 43:2; Lamentations 3:54) — the sense of being submerged, overwhelmed. "A burden too heavy to carry (massa kābēd)" — massāʾ (burden, load, oracle) is used for the prophetic oracle (Isaiah 13:1; Nahum 1:1) and for the physical burdens carried by the Levites in the wilderness (Numbers 4:15, 19, 24-32). The combination creates the picture of sin as an Oracle-burden — both heavy as a physical load and authoritative as a divine pronouncement against the speaker.
5: "My wounds stink and fester (hĕbîʾšû nāmaqû) because of my own foolishness (ʾiwwaltî)" — the language of festering wounds as a consequence of moral failure is both literal (the speaker is physically ill) and metaphorical (the wound-language carries the moral freight of "my sin stinks before God"). Nāmaq (to putrefy, to decay, to dissolve) is used for the decaying of the slain (Isaiah 34:4) and the dissolving of flesh (Zechariah 14:12). ʾIwwelet (foolishness) is the wisdom tradition's term for the character defect that leads to moral and practical destruction — the antithesis of ḥokmāh (wisdom). Significantly, the psalmist claims responsibility: "my own foolishness," not YHWH's arbitrariness.
6: "I am bowed low (naʿăwêṯî) and hunched over (šaḥōṯî); I walk about mourning (qōdēr) all day long" — the three postures of the penitent/afflicted are described. ʿĀwāh (to be bent, crooked, bowed) describes the physical manifestation of moral and physical burden. Šāḥāḥ (to bow down, to crouch, to be humiliated) — the verb for the posture of mourning, defeat, and prostration before God or king. Qādar (to be dark, to be in mourning dress, to go about darkly) — the wearing of dark mourning garments (sackcloth) and the resultant darkened appearance of one in mourning. All three terms were observable bodily states in the ancient world: the psalmist's inner condition was visible to the community through these postures and dress.
7: "My loins are on fire with disease (mālĕʾû qalĕqālōṯ), and there is no health anywhere in my body" — qālāl (to be degraded, to be shameful, here possibly "to be feverish/burning") is textually difficult. The loins (kĕsālîm) as the seat of physical strength and vitality (Psalms 66:11; Isaiah 21:3; Job 15:27) — the burning/disease in the loins signals the collapse of the core of physical power. "No health (ʾên tōm) anywhere in my body" — tōm (completeness, soundness, integrity) from tāmam (to be complete); the same word used for Job's "integrity" (Job 2:3, 9; Psalms 27:5). The psalmist's physical integrity has collapsed, mirroring the moral-spiritual collapse of Psalms 38:4-5.
8: "I am benumbed (pūgôṯî) and utterly crushed (nidkêṯî meʾōd); I cry out (šāʾagtî) from the anguish of my heart" — three states of extremity. Pûg (to be numb, to be cold, to faint) — used of the heart "growing cold" in dread (Genesis 45:26; Psalms 77:2). Dākaʾ (to be crushed, to be pulverized, to be ground down) — the most violent crushing, used for enemies crushed in battle (Psalms 143:3; Isaiah 53:5, 10) and for the brokenhearted whom God is near to (Psalms 34:18). Šāʾag (to cry out, to roar like a lion) — the most urgent, desperate vocal cry; used of God "roaring" in judgment (Amos 1:2; Joel 3:16) and of the psalmist in extremity (Psalms 22:1).
9: "Lord, all my longing (taʾăwāṯî) is laid out before you; my groaning (ʾanḥāṯî) is not hidden from you" — the turn from physical description to direct address. Taʾăwāh (longing, desire, appetite) — the same word for the food-longing of the wilderness wanderers (Numbers 11:4) and the object of Wisdom's desire (Proverbs 13:12). All the psalmist's longing is exposed before YHWH — not concealed or reordered into polite petition but "laid out" (negdĕkā, before your face). ʾAnḥāh (groaning, sighing) is the involuntary sound of suffering that exceeds words (Romans 8:26 — the Spirit intercedes with "groanings too deep for words" uses stenagmos, the LXX equivalent of ʾanḥāh).
10: "My heart pounds (sĕḥarḥar); my strength has failed, and even the light of my eyes has deserted me (ʾên ʾittî)" — sāḥar (to turn around, to revolve, to pound) in the intensive form suggests a heart racing and churning in agitation. "The light of my eyes" — a common idiom for vitality, consciousness, and the joy of living (Psalms 13:3; Psalms 19:8; Proverbs 15:30; 1 Samuel 14:27, 29). The phrase captures the complete spectrum of collapse: heart (emotional center), strength (physical capacity), and eyes (perceptual vitality) have all failed. The list of physical symptoms in Psalms 38:3-10 is one of the most comprehensive anatomical surveys of suffering in the Psalter.
11: "My friends and companions keep away (yirḥāqû) from my affliction, and even my relatives (qĕrôbāy) stand at a distance (mibbĕneged)" — the social isolation of illness in the ancient world was severe and systematic. In a culture where illness was interpreted as divine judgment, the sick person was socially dangerous — potentially contagious spiritually as well as physically. Levitical purity law required social isolation for various skin diseases (Lev 13-14), and general practice extended this to other severe illnesses. The psalmist's experience mirrors Job's: those who should provide comfort and solidarity — friends, companions, relatives — stand at a distance, abandoning the sick person precisely when human presence is most needed. Jesus's healing ministry was a deliberate reversal of this pattern.
12: "Those who want me dead set traps for me; those who seek to harm me speak of my destruction and scheme all day long" — while the psalmist is physically weakened and socially isolated (Psalms 38:11), the enemies are actively plotting. The trap metaphor (cf. Psalms 9:15; Psalms 35:7; Psalms 57:6) represents ambush-style conspiracy rather than open confrontation. The enemies's timing is strategic: they plot "all day long" (kol-hayyôm) while the psalmist is most vulnerable. This exploitation of illness and social isolation by enemies is a recognizable social pattern: the powerful attacking the sick was condemned by the prophets (Ezekiel 34:4 — shepherds who exploit the sick sheep rather than healing them).
13: "But I am like someone who cannot hear — I do not listen; like someone mute (ʾilēm) who cannot speak" — the psalmist's response to the plotting enemies is chosen silence — not inability but deliberate restraint. The deaf-and-mute posture is a strategy: by refusing to engage, the psalmist avoids being drawn into a contest he cannot win in his weakened state. Significantly, the same image of sheep-silence before the shearer (Isaiah 53:7) describes the Servant of the LORD: "he was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth." The resonance between the penitent psalmist and the silent Servant is developed in NT theology as Jesus's deliberate silence before his accusers (Mark 14:61; Psalms 15:5; Matthew 26:63).
14: "I am like a man who does not hear, with no counter-arguments (tôkāḥôṯ) in his mouth" — tôkēḥāh (proof, refutation, counter-argument) is the legal term for an evidence-based rebuttal: the psalmist has no legal defense prepared. In the ancient Israelite legal setting, the accused was expected to mount a verbal defense; the inability to do so was equivalent to admitting guilt. The psalmist chooses to bring no defense — not because he has no ground to stand on, but because he has laid all his case before YHWH (Psalms 38:9) rather than before human accusers. The divine court is the only tribunal that matters.
15: "But in you, LORD, I place my hope (yāḥaltî); you will answer (taʿăneh), O Lord my God" — the turn of the lament to trust. Yāḥal (to wait with hope, to hope with expectation) — the virtue of patient expectation (cf. Psalms 37:7 above). "You will answer" — the conviction that YHWH, unlike the human courts and social network, will respond. This confidence is not based on the speaker's innocence (he has confessed his sins, Psalms 38:3-4, 18) but on YHWH's covenant character as the one who hears prayer. The shift from the third-person description of enemies to the direct second-person address of YHWH in Psalms 38:15 is the pivot of the entire psalm.
16: "For I thought: Do not let them gloat over me — those who triumph (hăgdîlû) over me when I stumble" — the social-honor dimension: the enemies' goal is public triumph, the visible reversal of the psalmist's social standing. Gādal (to make great, to magnify, to triumph) in this context means the elevation of the enemy's status at the psalmist's expense — the ancient zero-sum honor game. The fear of the enemy's gloating was as real as the fear of physical harm: in an honor-shame culture, social humiliation before the community was a form of death. The psalmist prays to be spared both the physical fall (stumbling) and the social fall (enemies celebrating it).
17: "For I am about to stumble (lĕṣelāʿ nākon ʾānî) and my pain is always with me (wĕmaḵʾôbî negdî ṯāmîd)" — nākon (prepared, ready, set) with liṣlōaʿ (to stumble, to limp) — the psalmist is "prepared to stumble" — perched at the edge of collapse. "My pain is always before me (negdî)" — the pain is not intermittent or situational but negdî (before my face, constantly present in my perception). The chronic pain of Psalms 38:17 connects to the chronic scheming of the enemies in Psalms 38:12 ("all day long") — both the internal suffering and the external threat are unrelenting.
18: "I confess (ʾaggîd) my wrongdoing (ʿăwōnî); I am distressed (ʾedʾag) by my sin (mēḥaṭṭāʾṯî)" — the penitential confession at the center of the psalm. Nāgad (to declare, to report, to confess) in the hiphil is the act of making known what was hidden — the open declaration of sin rather than concealment. ʿĀwōn (iniquity, wrongdoing, guilt) — the broadest term for sin, covering both the act and its consequences. Ḥāṭṭāʾ (sin, failure, missing the mark) — the most common OT term, from ḥāṭāʾ (to miss). Together they cover the full range of human moral failure. "I am distressed by my sin" — the grief over sin is itself a component of repentance in the prophetic tradition (Joel 2:12-13; Isaiah 66:2).
19: "But my enemies are full of life (ḥayyîm) and strong (ʿāṣĕmû); those who hate me without cause (ḥinnām) have multiplied" — the cruel contrast of Psalms 38:19: while the psalmist is near death (Psalms 38:3-10), the enemies are "full of life (ḥayyîm)" — vigorous, healthy, powerful. ʿĀṣam (to be numerous, to be strong, to be mighty) — the same verb used for the multiplication of Israel in Egypt (Exodus 1:7, 20). "Without cause (ḥinnām)" — the gratuitous hostility (also Psalms 35:7, 19; Psalms 69:4; John 15:25 — Jesus applies ḥinnām to the hate of the world against him). The unjust prosperity of the enemy alongside the unjust suffering of the righteous is the theodicy tension the psalm refuses to resolve by denying either side.
20: "Those who repay good with evil (mĕšallĕmê rāʿāh taḥaṯ ṭôbāh) oppose me, because I pursue what is right" — the corruption of reciprocity is at its worst when good is repaid with evil. The ancient ANE reciprocity ethic made the exchange of good for evil the most severe breach of social covenant (cf. Proverbs 17:13; Genesis 44:4). "Because I pursue what is right (ʿal-rādĕpî-ṭôb)" — the irony is complete: the psalmist's goodness is the occasion of the attack. The righteous suffer precisely because their righteousness exposes and threatens the wicked. This dynamic — righteousness provoking enmity — is the pattern of the Suffering Servant (Isaiah 53:3-9) and is identified in the NT as the pattern of Christ's passion.
21: "Do not abandon me (al-taʿazbēnî), LORD; my God, do not stay far away (al-tirḥaq)" — the paired petitions of the lament's close: non-abandonment and proximity. Both petitions address the fear of divine withdrawal at the moment of greatest need. ʿĀzab (to abandon, to forsake, to leave) is the covenant term for the most severe divine relational act — used in Deuteronomy's warnings (Deuteronomy 31:6, 8; Joshua 1:5) and in the promises of Isaiah 41:17; Psalms 42:16; Psalms 49:15. The psalmist prays the covenant promise: "I will never leave you nor forsake you" (Deuteronomy 31:8). The proximity language ("do not be far") is paired with Jesus's cry of dereliction from Psalms 22:1: "my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" — the same language in the same moment of divine hiddenness.
22: "Come quickly to help me (ḥûšāh lĕʿezrāṯî), O Lord, my salvation (tĕšûʿāṯî)" — the urgent imperative "come quickly" (ḥûš, to hurry) is the emergency register of the lament petition — when time matters, when the situation is desperate (also Psalms 22:19; Psalms 40:13; Psalms 70:1, 5; Psalms 71:12; Psalms 141:1). "My salvation (tĕšûʿāṯî)" as a divine title — not "the God who saves" but "my salvation itself"; YHWH is identified with the rescue rather than merely its agent. The psalm that opened with a plea not to rebuke (Psalms 38:1) closes with a plea for urgent rescue — the movement from fear of divine anger to reliance on divine salvation is the trajectory of penitential prayer.
